does: This report examines how technical AI research can be steered toward reducing existential risks from artificial intelligence over the coming century. It introduces the concept of prepotence—a key property of hypothetical AI systems useful for categorizing potential existential risks—and uses this framework to analyze contemporary research directions. The authors evaluate multiple research areas through scenario-driven motivations, identifying which directions could positively contribute to existential safety while acknowledging that benefits depend crucially on how developments are deployed and implemented across society.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of AI safety literature that extends earlier risk analysis frameworks toward long-term horizons. It responds to prior existential risk scholarship by translating abstract safety concerns into concrete research directions grounded in current technical work. The analysis connects to earlier AI alignment research, robustness studies, and control-theoretic approaches while taking a broader view than many prior works by examining how diverse technical areas—from interpretability to governance—intersect with existential safety considerations. It synthesizes concerns raised across multiple research communities studying AI risks.
enables: This framework enables researchers across AI technical fields to evaluate their work through an existential safety lens, creating bridges between near-term capabilities research and long-term risk mitigation. By mapping research directions against existential safety criteria, it supports more intentional research prioritization and helps identify which emerging capabilities or theoretical advances might reduce versus increase existential risks. The prepotence concept and scenario-driven approach provide tools for ongoing evaluation as AI paradigms evolve, potentially guiding funding allocation, institutional research agendas, and interdisciplinary collaboration between technical AI researchers and those focused on safety, governance, and long-term human flourishing.
